note: Small in-process AVX2 warm-p50 test. Confirms the IVF latency win on AVX2 hardware (the measurement meh, pre-AVX2, could not produce). Release build, warm cache (3 throwaway queries before timing), 50 timed queries per probes via clock_timestamp() around ORDER BY emb <=> q LIMIT 10.

corpus.distribution: 200 latent clusters (one hot coord per cluster) + per-row jitter, L2-normalised

headline: probes=16 warm p50 0.78 ms vs probes=lists (full exact scan) 3.97 ms = 5.1x faster, on AVX2, release. The IVF cell-skipping latency win is real on AVX2 hardware.
caveats: recall@10 = 1.000 at EVERY probes (even probes=1) is an artifact of this synthetic corpus's strong cluster structure (each query's true neighbours all live in its own cell). It is NOT a general recall guarantee. On a harder distribution (e.g. the random-unit-vector corpus in ivf_recall_vs_probes_2026-06-16.json) recall climbs with probes as expected. This bench measures LATENCY honestly; for the recall/probes trade-off see the host-independent frontier.
200k x 256-d is a small corpus; absolute p50s would be larger at 1M+ x 1024-d, but the probes-vs-full-scan RATIO (the IVF win) is the point.
floki exposes AVX2 (not AVX-512) to /proc/cpuinfo, so turbovec takes the AVX2 SIMD path -- representative of typical modern x86 server hardware.
Single-host, in-process, lightly-loaded dev box (not a dedicated isolated bench like the v1.9.1 arnold protocol). Numbers are indicative, not a published frontier.
